Title: quinnrenaghan/simple-tree-walking-interpreter-c - GitHub
URL: https://github.com/quinnrenaghan/simple_tree_walking_interpreter_c
Content: The evaluator recursively "walks" the AST that the parser created, does what each node represents, and returns the value. It also utilizes an environment where

Title: CS61A Proj 4 - Scheme Interpreter - Misaka's blog
URL: https://www.misaka-9982.com/2022/12/17/CS61A-Proj-4
Content: >>> expr = read_line('(+ 2 2)') >>> expr Pair('+', Pair(2, Pair(2, nil))) >>> scheme_eval(expr, create_global_frame())  4  """  # Evaluate atoms if scheme_symbolp(expr): return env.lookup(expr) elif self_evaluating(expr):  return expr  # All non-atomic expressions are lists (combinations) if not scheme_listp(expr): raise SchemeError('malformed list: {0}'.format(repl_str(expr))) first, rest = expr.first, expr.rest if scheme_symbolp(first) and first in scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMS: return scheme_forms.SPECIAL_FORMSfirst else:  # BEGIN PROBLEM 3 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" procedure = scheme_eval(first, env) args = rest.map(lambda operand: scheme_eval(operand, env)) return scheme_apply(procedure, args, env)  # END PROBLEM 3 def pair_to_list(pair): if pair is nil: return [] else: return [pair.first] + pair_to_list(pair.rest) ``` |. (define x (+ 1 2)) validate_form(expressions, 2, 2) # Checks that expressions is a list of length exactly 2  # BEGIN PROBLEM 4 "*** YOUR CODE HERE ***" value = scheme_eval(expressions.rest.first, env) env.define(signature, value)  return signature  # END PROBLEM 4 elif isinstance(signature, Pair) and scheme_symbolp(signature.first): # defining a named procedure e.g.

Title: Interpreters - CS61A Notes
URL: https://cs61a.rouxl.es/docs/week-12/content/interpreters
Content: Our `scheme_eval` function will choose its execution behaviour based on the expression form given to it:. * If there’s a symbol in the expression, try to look up the value in the frame. * All other *legal* expressions are represented as Scheme lists (this could be normal call expressions or special forms) - these are called combinations. If the first token (after the bracket) matches that of a *known* special form, we use a different evaluation procedure, else we just do the normal scheme special from evaluation procedure. Similarly to Python, Scheme variable looks at frames to find the values bound to symbols. When applying user-defined procedures, we need to create a new frame where the arguments of the functions are defined, and then makes it such that the parent of the frame is just the `env` attribute of the procedure (you will see this in the Scheme Project code).

Title: Scheme Interpreter | CS 61A Spring 2026
URL: https://cs61a.org/proj/scheme
Content: # Scheme Interpreter * <scheme.zip>. > * Submit with Phase 1 and Phase 2 complete by **Thursday 04/23** (worth 0.5 pt). In this project, you will use Python to implement an interpreter for a subset of the Scheme language. The subset of the language used in this project is described in the functional programming section of Composing Programs, as well as this language specification and built-in procedure reference. Examples from Chapters 1 and 2 are included as test cases for this project. Language features from Chapters 3, 4, and 5 are not part of this project, but of course you are welcome to extend your interpreter to implement more of the language. You can download all of the project code as a zip archive. * `scheme_forms.py`: Python functions for evaluating special forms in Scheme (such as `define`, `lambda`, `and`, `cond`, etc.). * `questions.scm`: Scheme procedures for you to implement.

Title: 20. Tree-Walk Interpreters
URL: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=bEnBLbVwmC4
Content: 20. Tree-Walk Interpreters
Nicolas “Norswap” Laurent
1310 subscribers
42 likes
2300 views
5 Apr 2021
Writing simple AST-based interpreters is pretty easy! In this video I go over the basics of doing so, and also give some more specific tips.

Code from the video: https://gist.github.com/norswap/9b8daf474996436601a4e79d3a913d57
